questions.question: A 62-year-old patient is on day 2 post-emergency laparotomy. She develops tachycardia at 126/min, blood pressure 86/52 mmHg, and haemoglobin 6.4 g/dL. hs-cTnT rises from 180 to 540 ng/L (99th percentile URL = 14 ng/L). There is no chest pain and no ST changes on ECG. What MI type is MOST likely?
options: A: Type 1 MI (atherothrombotic plaque disruption with intracoronary thrombosis)
B: Type 4a MI (peri-procedural after percutaneous coronary intervention)
C: Type 5 MI (associated with coronary artery bypass grafting)
D: Type 2 MI (myocardial O2 supply-demand imbalance)
E: Type 3 MI (sudden cardiac death before biomarkers obtained)
questions.explanation: Tachycardia, hypotension, and severe anaemia combine to create myocardial O2 supply-demand imbalance — the defining mechanism of Type 2 MI. No clinical evidence of plaque disruption is present. This distinction is critical in critical care: dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) is indicated only for Type 1 MI; in Type 2, treatment targets the underlying stressor (haemodynamic instability, anaemia). Type 4a MI requires troponin >5× 99th URL within 48 hours of PCI and is not applicable here.
questions.topic: Type 1 vs 2 MI ICU supply-demand DAPT differentiation

questions.question: A 68-year-old male has 90 minutes of chest pain. ECG shows 2.5mm ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF, 1.5mm ST depression in V1-V3, and reciprocal ST depression in aVL. Which additional leads should be recorded FIRST?
options: A: Repeat standard 12-lead ECG in 15 minutes
B: V7, V8, V9 (posterior chest wall leads)
C: Continuous 12-lead monitoring without additional leads
D: V3R and V4R (right precordial leads)
E: High lateral augmentation leads only (I, aVL)
questions.explanation: In inferior MI (STE in II, III, aVF), guidelines recommend V3R and V4R to identify concomitant right ventricular infarction — a diagnosis with critical management implications (avoid nitrates and diuretics; RV preload dependent). V7–V9 are indicated for suspected posterior MI in the context of circumflex occlusion and prominent anterior ST depression; with inferior MI the culprit is typically the RCA, making V3R/V4R the priority. In high-degree AV block complicating inferior MI with RV involvement, in-hospital mortality is 2.5 times higher than inferior MI without AV block.
questions.topic: inferior STEMI V3R V4R RV infarction posterior leads priority

questions.question: A 58-year-old presents with 90 minutes of crushing chest pain. ECG shows LBBB with concordant ST elevation ≥1mm in leads with positive QRS complexes. No prior ECG is available. What is the MOST appropriate next step?
options: A: Serial troponins and repeat ECG after 6 hours of monitoring
B: IV fibrinolytic therapy, as PCI is not the preferred strategy in LBBB
C: Urgent primary PCI strategy
D: Repeat ECG in 30 minutes before committing to a reperfusion strategy
E: Bedside echocardiography to confirm regional wall motion abnormality before PCI
questions.explanation: New or presumed new LBBB with ischaemic symptoms is a STEMI-equivalent indication for primary PCI. The absence of a prior ECG must NOT delay reperfusion when the clinical context is strongly suggestive — the module explicitly states: 'Lack of a previous ECG should not be a reason to withhold such therapy when the clinical setting suggests myocardial infarction.' Concordant STE ≥1mm (first Sgarbossa criterion) further supports acute occlusion. Awaiting echocardiography before treatment introduces dangerous delay. Fibrinolysis is inferior to PCI and indicated only when PCI is unavailable.
questions.topic: LBBB STEMI equivalent primary PCI Sgarbossa ischaemic symptoms

questions.question: An ICU patient has elevated hs-cTnI. Which of the following would NOT be consistent with a diagnosis of Type 2 MI?
options: A: Severe anaemia reducing myocardial oxygen delivery
B: Atherosclerotic plaque disruption with intracoronary thrombosis
C: Sustained tachyarrhythmia causing increased myocardial oxygen demand
D: Sustained hypotension in a patient with underlying three-vessel coronary artery disease
E: Coronary artery spasm reducing myocardial perfusion
questions.explanation: Type 2 MI is defined by myocardial ischaemia due to O2 supply-demand imbalance rather than plaque disruption. Atherosclerotic plaque disruption with thrombosis is the defining mechanism of Type 1 (spontaneous) MI. This distinction drives treatment: DAPT is indicated for Type 1 but not Type 2. Options A, B, C, and E are all recognised Type 2 mechanisms — tachyarrhythmia (increased demand), spasm and hypotension (reduced supply), and anaemia (reduced O2 delivery) — as listed in the Fourth Universal Definition's classification of supply-demand imbalance.
questions.topic: Type 2 MI supply-demand NOT plaque rupture Type 1 distinction

questions.question: A 61-year-old post-menopausal woman is admitted to ICU after a cardiac arrest occurring minutes after news of a bereavement. ECG shows ST elevation in V1-V4. Emergency coronary angiography reveals entirely normal coronary arteries. Bedside echocardiography shows apical ballooning with normal basal segment contractility. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?
options: A: Acute anterior STEMI due to coronary vasospasm (Type 2 MI)
B: MINOCA — Type 1 MI without obstructive coronary artery disease
C: Takotsubo (apical ballooning) syndrome
D: Brugada syndrome unmasked by catecholamine surge
E: Acute lymphocytic myocarditis with regional distribution
questions.explanation: Takotsubo syndrome is characterised by precordial ST elevation, apical ballooning with preserved basal contractility on echocardiography, normal coronary angiography, and a strong association with post-menopausal females and emotional or physical stress. It accounts for 1–2% of clinically suspected STEMI. Normal coronary angiography is the confirming finding, not an exclusionary one. MINOCA requires evidence of AMI (cTn rise/fall) without obstructive CAD but does not produce the characteristic apical-basal dissociation seen here. Brugada syndrome shows an RBBB-like STE in V1-V3 but is not triggered by emotional stress in this manner and would not show apical ballooning.
questions.topic: Takotsubo apical ballooning normal angiography post-menopausal stress STEMI mimic

questions.question: A 76-year-old male develops cardiogenic shock 5 hours after anterior STEMI. Echocardiography estimates 46% of LV myocardium as akinetic. No mechanical complications are identified. Which mechanism MOST accounts for the shock?
options: A: Papillary muscle rupture with acute severe mitral regurgitation
B: Cardiac tamponade from haemopericardium
C: Right ventricular infarction with low cardiac output
D: Post-MI ventricular septal defect
E: Extensive LV contractile failure due to ischaemic myonecrosis
questions.explanation: LV failure is the most common cause of cardiogenic shock in AMI, accounting for approximately 78.5% of cases. Loss of >40% of functional LV myocardium is typically required to precipitate CS in the absence of mechanical complications; this patient has 46% akinesis. Mechanical complications collectively account for <20% of CS (VSD 3.9%, ischaemic MR 6.9%, RV failure 2.8%, tamponade 1.4%), and none were found on echocardiography. Papillary muscle rupture is the nearest miss but would be visible on echocardiography as acute severe MR and is far less common.
questions.topic: cardiogenic shock LV failure 78% 40% myocardium mechanical complications incidence

questions.question: A 63-year-old male presents 19 hours after onset of severe central chest pain. hs-cTnT is 3 850 ng/L at 0h and 3 440 ng/L at 3h (URL = 14 ng/L). ECG shows Q waves in V1–V4 with T-wave inversion and J-point elevation. CK-MB is elevated and declining. What does this troponin pattern MOST indicate?
options: A: Stable chronic myocardial injury — no acute event has occurred
B: Non-cardiac troponin elevation — the magnitude of the delta is too small to be diagnostic
C: Acute MI on the downslope of the time-concentration curve consistent with late presentation
D: Type 2 MI with demand ischaemia — stable falling troponin distinguishes this from Type 1
E: The falling pattern rules out AMI, which requires a rising phase
questions.explanation: The Fourth Universal Definition requires a 'rising and/or falling pattern' — a falling pattern alone satisfies the criteria for acute MI. In late presenters (>12–18 hours), troponin may already have peaked and be declining. The module states: 'absence of significant changes does not rule out acute myocardial injury if the clinical context is compatible.' Here, Q waves and T-wave inversion support evolving infarction. The 'falling pattern rules out AMI' distractor is the classic misconception — the definition explicitly includes a falling-only trajectory for late presentations. Chronic injury would show a persistently stable, non-dynamic elevation over days, not this falling pattern in a late acute presentation.
questions.topic: falling troponin late presentation AMI downslope dynamic pattern diagnosis

questions.question: A 65-year-old male presents with atypical chest discomfort. ECG shows ST elevation of 1.8mm in lead V2 and 1.0mm in lead V3, with no changes in other leads. Which statement BEST describes these findings?
options: A: Meets criteria in V2 only (the ≥1.5mm threshold applies to women, not men over 40)
B: Meets STEMI criteria — immediate reperfusion should be initiated
C: Normal variant — ST elevation <2mm in anterior leads is physiologically normal in all men
D: Borderline — STEMI criteria are met in V3 (≥1mm applies in all leads including V2–V3)
E: Does NOT meet STEMI criteria for a male older than 40 years in leads V2 and V3
questions.explanation: In leads V2–V3, the sex- and age-adjusted threshold for men older than 40 years is ≥2mm (not the standard ≥1mm used in all other leads). Neither 1.8mm in V2 nor 1.0mm in V3 meets this threshold. Initiating reperfusion therapy for sub-threshold STE risks unnecessary harm. The distractor claiming the ≥1.5mm threshold applies to men over 40 confuses the thresholds: ≥1.5mm applies to women of any age in V2–V3, while for men ≤40 years the cut-point is ≥2.5mm. The distractor claiming the standard ≥1mm applies to V2–V3 is incorrect. These patients require serial ECG monitoring and troponin measurement.
questions.topic: STEMI STE V2-V3 men 2mm women 1.5mm age-adjusted

questions.question: Regarding the GRACE risk score for acute coronary syndrome, which of the following is NOT a component?
options: A: Serum creatinine concentration
B: ST-segment deviation on presenting ECG
C: Cardiac arrest at admission
D: Heart rate at presentation
E: Prior aspirin use within the previous 7 days
questions.explanation: The GRACE score comprises 8 variables: age, heart rate, systolic blood pressure, serum creatinine, Killip class, cardiac arrest at admission, elevation of markers of myocardial injury, and ST-segment deviation. Prior aspirin use within 7 days is a variable in the TIMI risk score for UA/NSTEMI — a common exam confusion. A GRACE score >140 identifies high risk of in-hospital mortality; 109–140 identifies intermediate risk. Options A, B, C, and E are all genuine GRACE components.
questions.topic: GRACE score components NOT aspirin TIMI confusion 8 variables

questions.question: A 70-year-old with inferior STEMI, treated successfully with primary PCI, develops acute haemodynamic deterioration on day 3. A new, loud holosystolic murmur with a palpable thrill is heard at the lower left sternal border. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?
options: A: Post-MI ventricular septal defect
B: Acute left ventricular free wall rupture with haemopericardium
C: Severe aortic stenosis decompensated by haemodynamic stress
D: Right ventricular infarction with tricuspid regurgitation
E: Papillary muscle rupture with acute severe mitral regurgitation
questions.explanation: A new holosystolic murmur at the lower left sternal border with a palpable thrill is the pathognomonic finding of a post-MI ventricular septal defect. VSD occurs with both anterior and inferior wall infarction and accounts for approximately 3.9% of cardiogenic shock after AMI. Papillary muscle rupture is the nearest miss: it also causes a new holosystolic murmur, but it is heard best at the apex, radiates to the axilla, and produces acute mitral regurgitation rather than a lower sternal border murmur with thrill. Free wall rupture presents with sudden tamponade physiology typically without a murmur.
questions.topic: VSD post-MI murmur lower sternal border papillary rupture differentiation

questions.question: A 55-year-old female presents with NSTEMI. She has ≥3 CHD risk factors, prior coronary stenosis >50%, ST deviation on admission ECG, and 3 anginal episodes in the prior 24 hours. hs-cTnI is elevated. She has NOT taken aspirin in the prior 7 days. What is her TIMI risk score for UA/NSTEMI?
questions.explanation: The TIMI UA/NSTEMI score assigns 1 point each for 7 variables: age ≥65y (No = 0), ≥3 CHD risk factors (Yes = 1), prior stenosis ≥50% (Yes = 1), ST deviation (Yes = 1), ≥2 anginal events in 24h (Yes = 1), elevated biomarkers (Yes = 1), aspirin use in prior 7 days (No = 0). Total = 5. A score of 5 corresponds to a 26.2% 14-day event rate (death, MI, or urgent revascularisation). A score of 4 is the common near-miss answer, typically from overlooking the prior coronary stenosis criterion. Aspirin non-use scores 0 points (the variable scores 1 if aspirin WAS taken, reflecting aspirin-refractory ischaemia).
questions.topic: TIMI NSTEMI score calculation 7 predictors 14-day events aspirin criterion

questions.question: Which of the following variables is included in the TIMI risk score for STEMI but NOT in the GRACE score?
options: A: Serum creatinine concentration
B: DM, hypertension, or angina history
C: Heart rate on presentation
D: Cardiac arrest at admission
E: Elevation of cardiac biomarkers
questions.explanation: The TIMI STEMI score includes prior history of diabetes, hypertension, or angina (1 point), along with age categories, SBP <100 (3 points), HR >100 (2 points), Killip class II–IV (2 points), weight <67kg, anterior STE or LBBB, and time to reperfusion >4h. Prior medical history is not in GRACE. Serum creatinine and cardiac arrest at admission are GRACE components. Elevated biomarkers is a TIMI NSTEMI-score item, not a GRACE component. Heart rate is in both GRACE and TIMI STEMI — not a differentiator. The overlap between GRACE and TIMI components is a frequent exam source of confusion.
questions.topic: TIMI STEMI components DM HTN angina history NOT GRACE differentiation

questions.question: A 67-year-old female presents with severe chest pain and haemodynamic compromise (SBP 78 mmHg). ECG shows ST depression ≥1mm in eight surface leads (inferolateral distribution) and ST elevation in aVR and V1. No ST elevation elsewhere. Which culprit territory does this ECG pattern MOST suggest?
options: A: Isolated left circumflex artery occlusion (posterior territory)
B: Left main coronary artery obstruction or severe three-vessel ischaemia
C: Proximal LAD occlusion (anterior STEMI equivalent)
D: Proximal right coronary artery occlusion (inferior territory)
E: First diagonal branch occlusion (anterolateral territory)
questions.explanation: ST depression ≥1mm in ≥8 surface leads combined with ST elevation in aVR and/or V1, especially with haemodynamic compromise, is the ECG signature of left main coronary artery obstruction, left main equivalent, or severe three-vessel ischaemia. This pattern is explicitly listed in guidelines as an atypical sign warranting an immediate PCI strategy. Proximal LAD occlusion typically produces anterior ST elevation rather than diffuse subendocardial depression — the widespread depression with aVR elevation reflects global subendocardial ischaemia. RCA and circumflex occlusions produce territory-specific ECG changes.
questions.topic: left main ECG ST depression aVR elevation multivessel

questions.question: A patient with inferior STEMI and successful primary PCI develops complete heart block and new right bundle branch block during the hospital admission. Which culprit coronary territory does this complication MOST suggest?
options: A: Left main artery with multi-territory ischaemia
B: Left circumflex artery — lateral territory
C: Left anterior descending artery — anterior STEMI territory
D: Posterior descending artery — isolated inferior territory
E: Proximal right coronary artery
questions.explanation: High-degree AV block complicating inferior MI — particularly with bundle branch block involvement — indicates proximal RCA occlusion with extensive ischaemia encompassing the right ventricle and AV nodal artery. The AV node is supplied by the RCA in approximately 90% of people (right-dominant circulation). The module notes that when high-degree AV block occurs in inferior MI, in-hospital mortality is 2.5 times higher than inferior MI without AV block. In contrast, LBBB complicating anterior MI implicates the LAD (extensive anterior damage). New RBBB in inferior MI specifically indicates proximal RCA involvement.
questions.topic: inferior STEMI AV block RBBB proximal RCA mortality 2.5x higher

questions.question: A 70-year-old patient in the ICU with severe pneumonia has hs-cTnI of 820 ng/L at 0h and 825 ng/L at 6h (URL = 14 ng/L). There is no chest pain and the ECG shows only sinus tachycardia. Which interpretation is MOST appropriate?
options: A: No cardiac evaluation is needed as mild troponin elevation is an expected and clinically insignificant finding in sepsis
B: A non-dynamic (stable) elevation is most consistent with chronic myocardial injury; acute MI by current definition requires a rising or falling pattern
C: This pattern is consistent with Type 2 MI since troponin is elevated in the context of sepsis-related demand
D: This pattern confirms acute MI — any elevation above URL with troponin in this range is diagnostic
E: This likely rules out myocardial injury; the values may reflect laboratory interference
questions.explanation: The Fourth Universal Definition requires a 'rising and/or falling pattern' to diagnose acute MI. A completely stable elevation is most consistent with chronic myocardial injury or persistent structural heart disease. This patient has myocardial injury (cTn > 99th URL) but not acute MI by definition. The Type 2 MI distractor is wrong because Type 2 MI still requires a dynamic pattern — a stable value does not meet this criterion regardless of context. The 'no evaluation needed' distractor is dangerous: elevated troponin in the ICU, even when appearing chronic, warrants monitoring, documentation of baseline, and evaluation to exclude treatable causes. Sepsis can cause genuine acute myocardial injury requiring vigilance.
questions.topic: stable troponin ICU chronic injury no dynamic pattern

questions.question: Regarding the classification and terminology of acute coronary syndromes (ACS):

statements.label: A
statements.text: Fibrinolytic therapy is indicated in both STEMI and NSTEMI to lyse intracoronary thrombus.

statements.label: B
statements.text: At initial presentation, STEMI and NSTEMI are distinguished primarily by ECG findings rather than troponin levels.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Unstable angina is defined by ischaemic symptoms without troponin elevation above the 99th percentile URL.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Approximately 50% of patients presenting with ACS have ST-segment elevation on initial ECG.

questions.topic: ACS ECG classification fibrinolysis NSTEMI UA troponin STE incidence
questions.explanation: A: False — Fibrinolytics are contraindicated in NSTEMI (active non-occlusive thrombus; risk of harm without benefit). B: True — The ECG-based classification (STE vs no-STE) drives initial treatment decisions at presentation, while troponin confirms myocardial necrosis retrospectively. C: True — UA is the ACS category where troponin remains below the 99th percentile URL. D: False — Only about 10% of ACS presentations involve ST-segment elevation, not 50%.

questions.question: Regarding the clinical classification of myocardial infarction (Fourth Universal Definition):

statements.label: A
statements.text: Dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) is recommended for both Type 1 and Type 2 MI.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Type 5 MI (CABG-associated) uses the same biomarker threshold as Type 4a MI.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Type 2 MI results from myocardial oxygen supply-demand imbalance rather than atherosclerotic plaque disruption.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Type 4a MI is defined by troponin elevation >5× the 99th percentile URL within 48 hours of percutaneous coronary intervention.

questions.topic: MI types DAPT Type 1 only 4a 5x CABG 10x
questions.explanation: A: False — DAPT is indicated only for Type 1 MI (atherothrombotic). Type 2 MI should be managed by addressing the underlying supply-demand stressor, not with DAPT. B: False — Type 5 (CABG-associated) MI requires troponin >10× the 99th percentile URL — twice the Type 4a threshold (>5×), not the same threshold. C: True — Type 2 MI is defined by ischaemia due to O2 supply-demand imbalance, not plaque disruption. D: True — Type 4a MI is defined by troponin elevation >5× the 99th percentile URL within 48 hours of PCI.

questions.question: Regarding cardiac troponin kinetics and measurement in suspected AMI:

statements.label: A
statements.text: High-sensitivity cTn assays can shorten the diagnostic window to 1–3 hours in many patients.

statements.label: B
statements.text: CK-MB is a more sensitive and specific biomarker for myocardial injury than cardiac troponin.

statements.label: C
statements.text: A falling troponin pattern in a patient presenting 19 hours after symptom onset is consistent with acute MI.

statements.label: D
statements.text: A single hs-cTn value above the 99th percentile URL is sufficient to confirm the diagnosis of acute MI without further sampling.

questions.topic: hs-cTn diagnostic window CK-MB inferior troponin single value insufficient
questions.explanation: A: True — hs-cTn assays reduce time to diagnosis to within 1–3 hours in many patients. B: False — Cardiac troponin (cTnI and cTnT) is both more sensitive and more specific for myocardial injury than CK-MB; CK-MB is also found in skeletal muscle and brain, reducing its specificity. C: True — Late presenters (>12–18h) may already be on the downslope; a falling pattern alone satisfies the AMI definition. D: False — AMI requires a rising and/or falling pattern — a single elevated value indicates myocardial injury but does not confirm acute MI by the Fourth Universal Definition.

questions.question: Regarding ECG interpretation in suspected AMI:

statements.label: A
statements.text: In leads V2–V3, the STEMI threshold for men older than 40 years is ≥2mm ST elevation.

statements.label: B
statements.text: The first ECG in suspected ACS should be obtained within 10 minutes of first medical contact.

statements.label: C
statements.text: New or presumed new LBBB with ischaemic symptoms warrants a primary PCI strategy, regardless of prior ECG availability.

statements.label: D
statements.text: A normal 12-lead ECG reliably excludes the diagnosis of acute myocardial infarction.

questions.topic: ECG STEMI criteria normal ECG not exclude LBBB minutes
questions.explanation: A: True — V2–V3 use sex- and age-adjusted thresholds (≥2mm men >40y; ≥2.5mm men ≤40y; ≥1.5mm women). B: True — This is the guideline-specified maximum time target from first medical contact to ECG acquisition. C: True — Prior ECG unavailability must not delay reperfusion when new or presumed new LBBB accompanies ischaemic symptoms. D: False — 'A normal ECG does not exclude AMI' — some infarcts (especially posterior and RCA territory) can produce minimal or no STE on a standard 12-lead. Serial ECGs and supplemental leads are essential when clinical suspicion is high.

questions.question: Regarding ECG localisation of AMI and supplemental lead recordings:

statements.label: A
statements.text: ST elevation ≥0.5mm in posterior leads V7–V9 is diagnostic of posterior (inferobasal) MI.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Right precordial leads V3R and V4R should be recorded in patients with anterior MI to identify right ventricular infarction

statements.label: C
statements.text: ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF is most commonly associated with right coronary artery (RCA) occlusion.

statements.label: D
statements.text: ST depression ≥1mm in ≥8 surface leads with ST elevation in aVR identifies an isolated proximal LAD occlusion.

questions.topic: ECG localisation RCA inferior posterior left main aVR V4R
questions.explanation: A: True — Posterior leads (V7-V9) detect inferobasal infarction not visible on standard leads. B: False — V3R/V4R are recorded for INFERIOR MI (RCA territory, where RV infarction commonly co-occurs), not anterior MI — anterior MI is an LAD-territory event unrelated to RV perfusion. C: True — Inferior leads (II, III, aVF) ST elevation localises to the RCA territory per standard ECG localisation. D: False — This specific ECG pattern (widespread inferolateral ST depression plus aVR/V1 elevation) identifies left main coronary artery obstruction, left main equivalent, or severe three-vessel ischaemia — not an isolated LAD occlusion, which typically produces anterior ST elevation instead.

questions.question: Regarding Takotsubo (apical ballooning) syndrome:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Takotsubo disproportionately affects post-menopausal females and is often triggered by physical or emotional stress.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Finding normal coronary arteries on angiography effectively excludes the diagnosis of Takotsubo.

statements.label: C
statements.text: The echocardiographic hallmark is apical wall motion abnormality with preserved basal segment contractility.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Takotsubo accounts for approximately 10–15% of clinically suspected STEMI

questions.topic: Takotsubo apical ballooning normal angiography confirms post-menopausal incidence STEMI
questions.explanation: A: True — Takotsubo disproportionately affects post-menopausal females and is often triggered by physical or emotional stress. B: False — Normal coronary angiography is the confirming diagnostic finding in Takotsubo, not an exclusionary one — the diagnosis requires precordial STE, apical ballooning with basal sparing, AND absence of obstructive CAD. C: True — The echocardiographic hallmark is apical wall motion abnormality (ballooning) with preserved basal segment contractility. D: False — Takotsubo accounts for approximately 1–2% of clinically suspected STEMI, not 10–15% — a significant overestimate.

questions.question: Regarding cardiogenic shock (CS) complicating acute MI:

statements.label: A
statements.text: In STEMI patients, cardiogenic shock most commonly presents at the time of hospital admission.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Cardiogenic shock complicates approximately 7–10% of STEMI cases.

statements.label: C
statements.text: LV failure is the most common underlying cause of CS in AMI, accounting for approximately 78% of cases.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Loss of >40% of functional LV myocardium is typically required for CS in the absence of mechanical complications.

questions.topic: cardiogenic shock STEMI incidence timing NOT admission LV 40%
questions.explanation: A: False — The median time from STEMI to the onset of cardiogenic shock is 5–6 hours after admission — shock often develops after initial presentation, not at the time of admission itself. B: True — Cardiogenic shock complicates approximately 7–10% of STEMI cases. C: True — LV failure is the most common underlying cause of CS in AMI, accounting for approximately 78% of cases. D: True — Loss of >40% of functional LV myocardium is typically required for CS in the absence of mechanical complications.

questions.question: Regarding mechanical complications of acute MI:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Papillary muscle rupture typically presents with a new apical holosystolic murmur due to acute mitral regurgitation.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Acute LV free wall rupture is characterised by gradual haemodynamic deterioration over 12–24 hours.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Post-MI ventricular septal defect presents with a new holosystolic murmur at the apex, often with a palpable thrill, indistinguishable from papillary muscle rupture

statements.label: D
statements.text: Right ventricular infarction causes decreased cardiac output rather than a hyperdynamic state.

questions.topic: papillary rupture VSD free wall RV infarction low CO
questions.explanation: A: True — Papillary muscle rupture is heard best at the apex, radiating to the axilla, reflecting acute mitral regurgitation. B: False — LV free wall rupture is a catastrophic event presenting with sudden, severe haemodynamic collapse and tamponade physiology — not gradual deterioration — requiring immediate surgical intervention. C: False — Post-MI VSD produces its holosystolic murmur (with thrill) at the LOWER LEFT STERNAL BORDER, not the apex — the apex/sternal-border distinction is exactly what differentiates it from papillary muscle rupture at the bedside, not a similarity between them. D: True — RV infarction causes decreased RV compliance and systolic dysfunction, leading to venous congestion and LOW cardiac output, the opposite of a hyperdynamic state.

questions.question: Regarding risk stratification scoring systems in ACS:

statements.label: A
statements.text: The TIMI risk score for UA/NSTEMI predicts 14-day risk of death, MI, or urgent revascularisation using 5 independent predictors

statements.label: B
statements.text: A GRACE score >140 identifies patients at intermediate (not high) risk of in-hospital mortality.

statements.label: C
statements.text: The CRUSADE bleeding score includes male sex as one of its 8 variables for NSTEMI in-hospital bleeding risk

statements.label: D
statements.text: The GRACE score includes age, heart rate, systolic BP, serum creatinine, Killip class, cardiac arrest, biomarker elevation, and ST deviation as its 8 components.

questions.topic: GRACE 140 high risk TIMI NSTEMI CRUSADE female bleeding
questions.explanation: A: False — The TIMI UA/NSTEMI score uses 7 independent predictors, not 5 (age ≥65, ≥3 CHD risk factors, prior stenosis ≥50%, ST deviation, ≥2 anginal events/24h, elevated biomarkers, prior aspirin use). B: False — A GRACE score >140 identifies HIGH risk of in-hospital mortality (not intermediate); 109–140 indicates intermediate risk. C: False — The CRUSADE score includes FEMALE sex (not male sex) as one of its 8 bleeding-risk variables, alongside baseline haematocrit, creatinine clearance, diabetes, PVD, HF signs, systolic BP, and heart rate. D: True — The GRACE score comprises these 8 variables: age, heart rate, systolic blood pressure, serum creatinine, Killip class, cardiac arrest at admission, elevation of markers of myocardial injury, and ST-segment deviation.

questions.question: Regarding the pathophysiology of thrombus formation in ACS and the pharmacological implications:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Total coronary artery occlusion by thrombus typically produces ST-segment depression on ECG, while partial occlusion produces ST-segment elevation

statements.label: B
statements.text: Platelet aggregation on ruptured plaque forms platelet-rich 'white thrombus,' which is then reinforced by fibrin and red cells forming 'red thrombus.'

statements.label: C
statements.text: Nitric oxide and pro-inflammatory cytokines contribute to enhanced vasoconstriction, worsening afterload in prolonged cardiogenic shock

statements.label: D
statements.text: Fibrinolytic agents are effective against platelet-rich 'white thrombus' but NOT against fibrin-rich 'red thrombus.'

questions.topic: white red thrombus fibrinolytics NOT antiplatelet NO cardiogenic shock vasodilation
questions.explanation: A: False — Total occlusion typically produces ST-segment ELEVATION (STEMI), while partial/non-occlusive thrombus produces ST depression or no elevation (NSTEMI/UA) — not the reverse. B: True — Platelet aggregation on ruptured plaque forms platelet-rich 'white thrombus,' subsequently reinforced by fibrin and red cells forming 'red thrombus.' C: False — Endothelial/inducible NO synthase and pro-inflammatory cytokines (peroxynitrite, interleukins, TNF) contribute to pathological VASODILATION (not vasoconstriction) as cardiogenic shock progresses, counterbalancing the earlier compensatory vasoconstriction phase. D: False — The relationship is reversed from this statement: fibrinolytic agents lyse fibrin-rich 'red thrombus' but are NOT active against platelet-rich 'white thrombus' — antiplatelet agents target white thrombus instead.

questions.question: A patient's ECG is recorded 3 months after an untreated myocardial infarction. Based on the evolutionary timeline shown in the figure, which panel best represents the expected chronic ECG appearance?
options: A: Panel B (Hyperacute) — tall, peaked T-waves remain the dominant finding
B: Panel A (Normal) — no residual ECG changes are expected this long after infarction
C: Panel C (Acute) — persisting ST-segment elevation with ongoing T-wave inversion
D: Panel D (Indeterminate) — active dynamic ST-segment changes are still expected at 3 months
E: Panel E (Old) — persisting pathological Q-waves with normalised ST-segments and T-waves
questions.explanation: Three months after an untreated infarct falls within the 'weeks to months' timeframe of the Old stage: persisting pathological Q-waves with normalised ST-segments and T-waves, reflecting a stable, fully evolved scar. The Normal panel shows no infarct changes at all. The Hyperacute and Acute panels reflect the first minutes to hours, with active ST-segment/T-wave changes that would have long since resolved by 3 months. The Indeterminate panel (days to weeks) still shows persisting T-wave inversion, which would be expected to have resolved by the Old stage.
questions.topic: ECG evolution stages hyperacute acute early indeterminate old timeline Q-wave

questions.question: The figure shows a 12-lead ECG obtained in a patient with acute chest pain. Based on the pattern of ST-segment changes shown, which coronary territory is most likely affected?
options: A: Inferior territory — RCA or posterolateral branch of the circumflex
B: Anterolateral territory — proximal LAD involving diagonal branches
C: Anterior territory — proximal LAD occlusion
D: Isolated right ventricular territory without inferior involvement
E: Lateral territory — isolated circumflex occlusion
questions.explanation: The ECG shows ST-segment elevation in the inferior leads (II, III, aVF), the characteristic pattern of inferior wall infarction, most commonly due to occlusion of the right coronary artery (RCA) or, less commonly, the posterolateral branch of the circumflex artery. Anterior and anterolateral patterns (LAD territory) would show ST elevation in the precordial leads (V2-V4) and lateral patterns in I, aVL, V5-V6, none of which are the dominant finding here. Right precordial leads (V3R-V4R) should be recorded next to assess for concomitant right ventricular infarction, a recognised complication of proximal RCA occlusion.
questions.topic: inferior MI ECG recognition RCA localisation ST elevation II III aVF

questions.question: A patient presents with acute chest pain. The limb leads of the 12-lead ECG are shown in the figure. Which of the following best describes the QRS morphology demonstrated?
options: A: Normal sinus rhythm with a normal QRS duration and morphology
B: Right bundle branch block — broad QRS with a characteristic rSR' pattern in right precordial leads
C: Complete heart block with atrioventricular dissociation
D: Left bundle branch block — broad QRS with an abnormal depolarisation sequence
E: Ventricular pre-excitation (Wolff-Parkinson-White pattern) with a delta wave
questions.explanation: The limb leads show broad QRS complexes with the characteristic left bundle branch block morphology (broad, often notched R-wave in lead I, deep S-wave in the inferior leads), reflecting delayed left ventricular depolarisation via the right bundle. New or presumed new LBBB with ischaemic symptoms is managed as a STEMI-equivalent, since the lack of a prior ECG for comparison should not delay reperfusion therapy when the clinical context is strongly suggestive of infarction. RBBB produces a different morphology (rSR' pattern, best seen in right precordial leads not shown here), WPW shows a delta wave with a short PR interval, and complete heart block shows dissociated P-waves and QRS complexes — none of which match this tracing.
questions.topic: LBBB morphology recognition ECG limb leads broad QRS bundle branch block

questions.question: The figure shows the time course of several biochemical markers following acute myocardial infarction, expressed as multiples of the upper limit of normal. A patient develops recurrent chest pain and ECG changes 5 days after an initial MI. Based on the curves shown, which marker would be MOST useful for confirming re-infarction at this time point, given it should have already returned towards baseline from the index event?
options: A: Troponin T, since it remains markedly elevated through day 7 and cannot distinguish new from ongoing injury
B: Troponin I, since it remains elevated throughout the first week for the same reason as troponin T
C: CK-MB, since it has typically returned to near-baseline by day 3-4, making a new rise more specific for re-infarction
D: LDH, since it is the most cardiac-specific of the markers shown
E: AST, since it remains the most persistently elevated marker throughout the week shown
questions.explanation: CK-MB returns to near-baseline within approximately 2-3 days (by the time-course shown), in contrast to troponin T and troponin I, which remain elevated for up to a week or more after the index event. This more rapid resolution makes a new rise in CK-MB more specific for detecting reinfarction occurring several days after an initial MI, since persistently-elevated troponin cannot easily distinguish ongoing/resolving injury from a new event. AST is less cardiac-specific (also present in liver and skeletal muscle) and is not used for this purpose in modern practice. LDH remains elevated for a similarly prolonged period to troponin and lacks cardiac specificity.
questions.topic: biomarker kinetics CK-MB troponin reinfarction detection curve comparison timing

questions.question: A patient presents with acute chest pain. Serial high-sensitivity troponin measurements at 0h and 3h both remain within the normal reference range, and the ECG shows no ischaemic changes. Bedside echocardiography is performed. Which statement best describes the correct interpretation of this combination of findings?
options: A: Echocardiography and troponin measurement carry equal diagnostic weight and neither takes precedence over the other
B: Normal serial troponin measurements at appropriately timed intervals exclude acute MI and take precedence over echocardiographic findings
C: A small regional wall motion abnormality on echocardiography would override normal troponin results and mandate a diagnosis of acute MI
D: Echocardiography should be performed before biomarkers are drawn, since imaging is more sensitive for early infarction
E: A normal echocardiogram with normal wall motion is required to definitively exclude acute MI, regardless of the biomarker results
questions.explanation: When cardiac biomarkers have been sampled at appropriately timed intervals and remain normal, this excludes acute MI and takes precedence over echocardiographic findings — biomarker evidence is the primary diagnostic criterion in the Fourth Universal Definition. Echocardiography's strength lies in detecting regional wall motion abnormalities and mechanical complications, and in excluding alternative diagnoses (aortic dissection, pericardial effusion, severe aortic stenosis), but a small MI can escape detection by echocardiography even though normal biomarkers reliably exclude significant infarction. Echocardiography is not required before biomarker sampling, and an isolated wall motion abnormality without appropriately-timed biomarker confirmation would not override normal serial troponin results.
questions.topic: echocardiography troponin precedence normal biomarkers exclude MI imaging criteria

questions.question: A 68-year-old woman with known ischaemic cardiomyopathy (LVEF 25%) presents to the ICU with severe dyspnoea. BP 174/96 mmHg, HR 114 bpm, SpO2 87% on high-flow oxygen, bibasal crepitations to mid-zones, raised JVP, and warm peripheries. According to ESC guidelines, what is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological strategy?
options: A: IV norepinephrine and vasopressin
B: Oral bisoprolol titration
C: IV dobutamine infusion
D: IV levosimendan infusion
E: IV furosemide with IV glyceryl trinitrate
questions.explanation: This is a 'wet-warm' presentation — congestion with adequate peripheral perfusion and hypertension. ESC guidelines recommend IV loop diuretics as first-line (to reduce congestion), with vasodilators as beneficial adjuncts in hypertensive patients. Inotropes (levosimendan, dobutamine) are reserved for the 'cold' cardiogenic shock phenotype. Starting or titrating beta-blockers in acute decompensation reduces cardiac output and worsens the clinical state.
questions.topic: AHF wet-warm ESC treatment algorithm diuretics vasodilators

questions.question: A 61-year-old man is admitted with inferior STEMI. He has mild tachycardia (HR 102 bpm), bibasal crepitations to the lower zones, a third heart sound, but no florid pulmonary oedema and no haemodynamic compromise (BP 118/72 mmHg). What is his Killip-Kimball class and associated approximate 30-day mortality?
options: A: Class II — <17%
B: Class I — <6%
C: Class IV — 81%
D: Class II — 38%
E: Class III — 38%
questions.explanation: Killip Class II = heart failure with signs of congestion (bibasal crepitations, 3rd heart sound) without florid pulmonary oedema, carrying <17% 30-day mortality. Class III = florid pulmonary oedema throughout all lung fields (~38%). The 'Class II — 38%' distractor is the near-miss trap — correct class identification but wrong mortality; candidates who know 'Class II' without knowing the mortality threshold will select it.
questions.topic: Killip-Kimball classification AMI mortality criteria

questions.question: A 74-year-old man with known ischaemic cardiomyopathy develops worsening dyspnoea and hypoxaemia (SpO2 88%, PaCO2 4.6 kPa) despite high-flow oxygen. You commence non-invasive ventilation. Which statement most accurately reflects current evidence for NIV mode selection in acute heart failure?
options: A: CPAP should be avoided in AHF as it increases LV transmural pressure and worsens afterload
B: BiPAP is contraindicated in AHF when PaCO2 is normal
C: CPAP is contraindicated with severely impaired LV function (EF <20%) due to haemodynamic compromise
D: BiPAP is preferred as it has demonstrated superior reduction in mortality over CPAP in randomised trials
E: CPAP and BiPAP are both appropriate; randomised data have not shown BiPAP to be superior to CPAP in AHF
questions.explanation: Multiple RCTs show BiPAP improves CO2 clearance but has NOT demonstrated superiority over CPAP for mortality or intubation rates in AHF. Both modes reduce work of breathing and improve oxygenation. CPAP reduces LV afterload by decreasing ventricular transmural pressure — the distractor claiming CPAP should be avoided because it worsens afterload reverses this physiology. Neither mode is contraindicated on the basis of EF or PaCO2 alone.
questions.topic: NIV CPAP BiPAP acute heart failure evidence comparison

questions.question: A 58-year-old man with anterior STEMI and mild hypoxaemia (SpO2 94%) is on 15L/min O2 via non-rebreather mask, achieving SpO2 100%. A colleague argues this is beneficial. Which best describes the cardiovascular effects of hyperoxia in this context?
options: A: SpO2 ≥98% is recommended by ESC guidelines in all patients with acute myocardial infarction
B: Hyperoxia improves coronary vasomotor tone and reduces myocardial oxygen demand
C: Hyperoxia is beneficial in cardiogenic shock as increased dissolved oxygen compensates for reduced cardiac output
D: The haemodynamic effects of hyperoxia are only clinically relevant when PaO2 exceeds 60 kPa
E: Hyperoxia causes coronary vasoconstriction and impairs ventricular contractility, worsening outcomes in acute coronary syndrome
questions.explanation: Hyperoxia causes coronary vasoconstriction, reduces coronary blood flow, and is associated with impaired ventricular contractility and worse outcomes in ACS. The pragmatic target is SpO2 ≥90% — correcting hypoxaemia is appropriate but overcorrection is harmful. Dissolved oxygen contributes negligibly to oxygen delivery at physiological atmospheric pressure, contradicting the claim that it can compensate for reduced cardiac output in cardiogenic shock, and there is no ESC recommendation for SpO2 ≥98% in non-hypoxaemic ACS patients.
questions.topic: hyperoxia harm coronary vasoconstriction ACS SpO2 target

questions.question: A 65-year-old man with cardiogenic shock following anterior STEMI is assessed for IABP insertion. TTE reveals moderate-to-severe aortic regurgitation in addition to severely impaired LV function (EF 15%). Why does the aortic regurgitation represent a contraindication to IABP?
options: A: AR limits IABP efficacy only through reduced diastolic blood pressure, not through LV volume overload
B: The IABP balloon physically obstructs regurgitant flow across the aortic valve
C: IABP diastolic inflation increases aortic diastolic pressure, augmenting regurgitant flow and LV volume overload
D: Aortic regurgitation is only a contraindication to Impella, not to IABP
E: Moderate AR is not a contraindication; only severe (grade IV) AR precludes IABP use
questions.explanation: IABP inflates in diastole, augmenting aortic diastolic pressure. In aortic regurgitation, this increases the driving pressure for regurgitant flow back into the LV, worsening LV volume overload. The module specifies moderate (or greater) AR as the threshold — the distractor claiming only severe AR precludes IABP is the near-miss trap, since the actual threshold is moderate-or-greater.
questions.topic: IABP contraindication aortic regurgitation diastolic augmentation mechanism

questions.question: A 40-year-old woman with fulminant myocarditis and refractory cardiogenic shock is placed on peripheral femoro-femoral VA-ECMO. Despite haemodynamic stabilisation, repeat TTE 12 hours later shows a severely dilated LV with markedly elevated end-diastolic pressure and worsening pulmonary oedema. Which best explains this finding?
options: A: Peripheral VA-ECMO reduces systemic venous return, causing reduced LV preload and secondary dilatation
B: The membrane oxygenator generates reactive oxygen species that directly impair myocardial contractility
C: VA-ECMO causes LV dilatation by over-filling the left ventricle via the membrane oxygenator
D: VA-ECMO bypasses the left heart by drawing blood directly from the pulmonary circulation
E: Peripheral VA-ECMO returns blood retrograde into the aorta, increasing LV afterload without unloading the LV
questions.explanation: Peripheral VA-ECMO returns oxygenated blood retrograde into the aorta, increasing arterial elastance and LV afterload. Unlike Impella or TandemHeart — which directly unload the LV by drawing blood from the ventricle — peripheral VA-ECMO does not vent the LV. The net effect may be increased LV stroke work and myocardial oxygen consumption. LV venting strategies (IABP, Impella, surgical vent) are often required alongside VA-ECMO.
questions.topic: VA-ECMO peripheral LV afterload no unloading pulmonary oedema paradox

questions.question: A 29-year-old previously healthy man presents with fulminant lymphocytic myocarditis and refractory cardiogenic shock. Cardiac MRI shows extensive myocardial oedema without fibrosis. Short-term MCS is initiated. Which MCS strategy designation is most appropriate given the clinical context?
options: A: Bridge to bridge
B: Bridge to transplant
C: Destination therapy
D: Bridge to recovery
E: Bridge to decision
questions.explanation: Bridge to recovery = short-term MCS to stabilise haemodynamics until cardiac function recovers in patients with potentially reversible myocardial dysfunction. Fulminant myocarditis in a young patient without fibrosis has good recovery potential. 'Bridge to decision' is the near-miss distractor — it is used when suitability for transplantation or long-term MCS is uncertain, not when recovery is the primary expectation. Destination therapy is for patients ineligible for transplantation.
questions.topic: MCS strategy bridge to recovery myocarditis reversible

questions.question: A 58-year-old man with refractory cardiogenic shock secondary to STEMI is on VA-ECMO. A trainee calculates a low SAVE score and suggests withdrawing ECMO on grounds of futility. Which statement most accurately reflects the intended use of the SAVE score?
options: A: Higher SAVE scores indicate greater predicted in-hospital mortality
B: The SAVE score was validated in a single-centre European cohort and has limited applicability beyond that population
C: A low SAVE score mandates ECMO withdrawal discussion at 48 hours
D: The SAVE score was designed for the general critically ill population and predicts 30-day survival independent of ECMO
E: The SAVE score predicts in-hospital survival in cardiogenic shock on VA-ECMO; it should not determine individual futility or replace clinical judgement
questions.explanation: The SAVE score predicts in-hospital survival specifically for cardiogenic shock patients on VA-ECMO (developed via ELSO/Alfred Hospital, multinational cohort). Developers explicitly state it should not substitute clinical judgement or determine individual futility. Higher SAVE scores predict higher survival, not mortality — the distractor claiming higher scores indicate greater mortality reverses this. The external validation AUC was 0.9, contradicting the claim of limited applicability beyond a single-centre European cohort.
questions.topic: SAVE score cardiogenic shock VA-ECMO survival prediction futility limitation

questions.question: A 62-year-old woman, 3 days post-cardiac surgery, develops bilateral pulmonary infiltrates, severe hypoxaemia (P:F ratio 90), and worsening respiratory mechanics. Temperature is 38.6°C. Echocardiographic windows are poor. It is clinically unclear whether this represents cardiogenic pulmonary oedema or ARDS. Which monitoring strategy is most useful to discriminate between these diagnoses?
options: A: Minimally invasive cardiac output monitoring (e.g., PiCCO) provides equivalent diagnostic discrimination
B: High-resolution CT of the thorax
C: Repeat echocardiography with IV contrast
D: Pulmonary artery catheterisation (PAC), enabling measurement of PCWP and mixed venous oxygen saturation
E: Serial BNP measurements every 4 hours
questions.explanation: PAC enables direct measurement of PCWP (proxy for LV filling pressure): elevated PCWP (>18 mmHg) supports cardiogenic oedema; low PCWP with clinical infection supports ARDS. SvO2 additionally guides adequacy of oxygen delivery. Although the PAC-MAN trial showed no survival benefit with PAC in the general ICU population, discriminating between cardiogenic and non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema with inadequate echo windows is a recognised specific indication. PiCCO estimates cardiac output but cannot directly measure filling pressures with the same reliability as PAC.
questions.topic: PAC PCWP cardiogenic ARDS pulmonary oedema discrimination monitoring

questions.question: A 68-year-old patient with ischaemic cardiomyopathy on long-term carvedilol develops acute decompensated heart failure with cardiogenic shock (CI 1.6 L/min/m², lactate 4.8 mmol/L). Despite IV furosemide and fluid optimisation, he remains cold and hypoperfused. Which inotrope's efficacy is LEAST likely to be attenuated by chronic carvedilol use?
options: A: Dobutamine
B: Levosimendan
C: Dopamine
D: Isoprenaline
E: Adrenaline (epinephrine)
questions.explanation: Levosimendan increases myocardial contractility by sensitising cardiac troponin C to calcium ions, independent of beta-adrenoceptor activation. Chronic beta-blocker use and consequent receptor down-regulation do not attenuate its inotropic effect. All other options (dobutamine, adrenaline, dopamine, isoprenaline) act through beta-adrenoceptors and may have reduced efficacy in the context of chronic beta-blockade. Levosimendan is also a potent vasodilator via vascular K+ channel activation.
questions.topic: levosimendan troponin C calcium sensitiser beta-blocker independent inotrope

questions.question: A 62-year-old man with BMI 46 kg/m² presents with dyspnoea. BNP returns at 68 pg/mL (local threshold for HF unlikely: <100 pg/mL). A colleague concludes that heart failure is excluded. What is the most important reason to question this conclusion?
options: A: Obesity is associated with lower circulating BNP levels, risking a falsely reassuring result despite true heart failure
B: BNP <100 pg/mL has 100% sensitivity and specificity for excluding heart failure in all populations
C: BNP is often elevated in obese patients, making the threshold unreliable in the opposite direction
D: In obese patients, only NT-proBNP should be used; BNP testing is invalid
E: BNP levels are unaffected by BMI and the clinical threshold remains valid regardless of body weight
questions.explanation: Obesity is associated with reduced circulating BNP levels, likely due to increased clearance via natriuretic peptide receptors expressed on adipose tissue. This means standard BNP thresholds may yield false-negative results in obese patients. The distractor claiming BNP is elevated in obesity reverses the true direction of the association. Clinical assessment and echocardiography remain essential when suspicion is high despite a low BNP.
questions.topic: BNP falsely low obesity false negative heart failure diagnosis

questions.question: A 69-year-old man with acute decompensated heart failure has received IV furosemide 500 mg/24h for 3 days. Urine output is 180 mL/24h. Serum K+ is 6.9 mmol/L, creatinine 368 µmol/L, and he has worsening pulmonary oedema. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: A: Increase IV furosemide to 1000 mg/24h
B: Initiate levosimendan to improve renal perfusion and restore diuretic responsiveness
C: Commence renal replacement therapy with ultrafiltration
D: Add IV acetazolamide to overcome loop diuretic resistance
E: Administer IV sodium bicarbonate to improve diuretic response
questions.explanation: This patient has multiple concurrent absolute indications for RRT: diuretic-refractory volume overload with pulmonary oedema, severe hyperkalaemia (K+ >6.5 mmol/L), and rising creatinine indicating AKI. Ultrafiltration/haemofiltration effectively reduces circulating volume, lowers LVEDP, and corrects electrolyte abnormalities. Escalating the diuretic dose in established diuretic resistance is ineffective and risks further AKI. Levosimendan may improve cardiac output but does not reliably and urgently address severe life-threatening hyperkalaemia.
questions.topic: RRT indication diuretic refractory volume overload hyperkalaemia AKI

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a contraindication or significant limitation to intra-aortic balloon pump (IABP) insertion?
options: A: Known aortic dissection involving the descending thoracic aorta
B: Cardiogenic shock refractory to inotropes and vasopressors
C: Moderate-to-severe aortic regurgitation
D: Rapid atrial fibrillation (ventricular rate 140–160 bpm)
E: Severe peripheral vascular disease with absent femoral pulses bilaterally
questions.explanation: Cardiogenic shock refractory to medical therapy is an INDICATION for IABP, not a contraindication. Moderate-to-severe AR is a contraindication (diastolic inflation worsens regurgitation). Severe PVD risks limb ischaemia. Rapid AF significantly reduces IABP efficacy as insufficient diastolic time is available for augmentation — while not an absolute contraindication, it substantially limits benefit. Aortic dissection involving the descending aorta is an anatomical contraindication.
questions.topic: IABP NOT contraindication cardiogenic shock indication limitation

questions.question: A 57-year-old woman with acute decompensated heart failure has developed RV failure (CVP 22 mmHg, dilated RV on echo, estimated PVR 6.8 WU on PAC). Despite dobutamine, RV function continues to deteriorate. An inotrope with pulmonary vasodilatory properties is needed. Which drug class best provides this combination?
options: A: PDE-3 inhibitors (e.g., milrinone) increase myocardial cAMP, boosting contractility and causing pulmonary vasodilation
B: Digoxin — via Na/K-ATPase inhibition with selective pulmonary vasodilation
C: Beta-2 agonists (e.g., salbutamol) — via selective pulmonary vasoconstriction and positive inotropy
D: Vasopressin — via V1-receptor-mediated selective pulmonary vasodilation
E: Catecholamines (e.g., adrenaline) — via alpha-1-mediated pulmonary vasoconstriction
questions.explanation: PDE-3 inhibitors (milrinone, enoximone) inhibit phosphodiesterase-3, increasing myocardial cAMP and contractility independent of beta-adrenoceptors. Elevated vascular smooth muscle cAMP causes vasodilation — particularly pronounced in the pulmonary circulation — making them valuable in RV failure with elevated PVR. The main clinical risk is systemic hypotension. Catecholamines do not selectively dilate pulmonary vasculature; vasopressin causes pulmonary vasoconstriction (not dilation); digoxin has no relevant pulmonary vasodilatory effect.
questions.topic: PDE3 inhibitor milrinone pulmonary vasodilation RV failure inotrope

questions.question: In the SAVE score for cardiogenic shock patients on VA-ECMO, which of the following is NOT associated with lower survival (i.e., is a PROTECTIVE factor predicting better outcomes)?
options: A: Chronic renal failure prior to ECMO initiation
B: Pre-ECMO cardiac arrest
C: Established multi-organ failure before ECMO commencement
D: Refractory VT/VF as the primary indication for VA-ECMO
E: Prolonged mechanical ventilation prior to ECMO initiation
questions.explanation: Refractory VT/VF as the indication for VA-ECMO is a PROTECTIVE factor in the SAVE score (associated with better survival), likely reflecting potentially reversible arrhythmic aetiology and less antecedent comorbidity compared to structural cardiomyopathy. All other listed factors — chronic renal failure, pre-ECMO cardiac arrest, prolonged pre-ECMO mechanical ventilation, and established multi-organ failure — are adverse factors associated with lower SAVE scores and worse in-hospital survival.
questions.topic: SAVE score protective factors VT VF NOT poor outcome ECMO

questions.question: Regarding neurohormonal responses and haemodynamic changes in acute heart failure:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Sympathetic nervous system activation initially compensates for reduced cardiac output by increasing heart rate and myocardial contractility.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Persistent RAAS activation is ultimately deleterious, driving progressive fluid retention, vasoconstriction, and adverse cardiac remodelling.

statements.label: C
statements.text: The primary mechanism of pulmonary oedema in left heart failure is direct inflammatory injury to the alveolar-capillary membrane.

statements.label: D
statements.text: In the LV pressure-volume loop, the area enclosed by the loop represents stroke volume.

questions.topic: neurohormonal RAAS sympathetic PV loop stroke volume area
questions.explanation: A: True — SNS activation initially compensates for reduced cardiac output by increasing heart rate and contractility. B: True — Persistent RAAS activation is ultimately deleterious, driving fluid retention, vasoconstriction, and adverse remodelling. C: False — Pulmonary oedema results from increased intravascular pulmonary pressures driving transudation of protein-depleted plasma, not inflammatory injury (which is the ARDS mechanism). D: False — The enclosed loop AREA represents myocardial oxygen consumption (stroke work + potential energy); stroke volume is the WIDTH (EDV minus ESV).

questions.question: Regarding the Killip-Kimball classification in post-AMI acute heart failure:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Killip Class IV (cardiogenic shock) is associated with approximately 50% 30-day mortality.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Killip Class III is defined by florid pulmonary oedema and carries approximately 38% 30-day mortality.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Killip Class I is characterised by a third heart sound and bibasilar crepitations, with an associated 30-day mortality below 6%.

statements.label: D
statements.text: The Killip-Kimball classification was primarily designed and validated in non-ischaemic cardiomyopathy.

questions.topic: Killip-Kimball AMI mortality class criteria cardiogenic shock
questions.explanation: A: False — Class IV carries approximately 81% 30-day mortality, not 50%; the 50% figure is a common underestimate trap. B: True — Class III = florid pulmonary oedema, carrying approximately 38% 30-day mortality. C: False — A third heart sound and bibasilar crepitations describe Class II, not Class I (Class I = no clinical signs of heart failure); the <6% mortality figure belongs to Class I, so the statement mismatches its own criteria and mortality. D: False — The score was designed specifically for heart failure following AMI and validated in STEMI/NSTEMI populations.

questions.question: Regarding the intra-aortic balloon pump (IABP) in cardiogenic shock:

statements.label: A
statements.text: The IABP-SHOCK II trial demonstrated a significant reduction in 30-day mortality from IABP in AMI-related cardiogenic shock, resulting in a Class I ESC recommendation.

statements.label: B
statements.text: IABP inflation during diastole augments aortic diastolic pressure and increases coronary artery perfusion pressure.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Moderate or greater aortic regurgitation is a contraindication to IABP insertion.

statements.label: D
statements.text: IABP provides greater LV stroke work reduction than the Impella or TandemHeart devices.

questions.topic: IABP haemodynamic effects SHOCK II trial ESC Class III AR contraindication
questions.explanation: A: False — IABP-SHOCK II showed NO mortality benefit in AMI-related cardiogenic shock; ESC 2016 downgraded IABP to Class III (not routinely recommended). B: True — Diastolic inflation augments aortic diastolic pressure and coronary perfusion; systolic deflation reduces afterload. C: True — Diastolic inflation worsens the regurgitant volume in aortic regurgitation, so moderate-or-greater AR is a contraindication. D: False — IABP provides only modest LV benefit; Impella and TandemHeart achieve significantly greater reductions in LV stroke work and myocardial oxygen consumption on PV loop analysis.

questions.question: Regarding veno-arterial ECMO (VA-ECMO) in cardiogenic shock:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Peripheral VA-ECMO reliably unloads the left ventricle by reducing LV end-diastolic pressure and wall stress.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Peripheral VA-ECMO provides both biventricular haemodynamic support and respiratory gas exchange.

statements.label: C
statements.text: VA-ECMO is an established long-term device suitable as 'destination therapy' for patients ineligible for cardiac transplantation.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Moderate or greater aortic regurgitation is a contraindication to VA-ECMO.

questions.topic: VA-ECMO LV afterload unloading AR contraindication destination therapy
questions.explanation: A: False — Peripheral VA-ECMO returns blood retrograde into the aorta, increasing LV afterload; it may worsen LV wall stress and typically requires additional LV venting. B: True — Peripheral VA-ECMO provides biventricular support plus gas exchange via the membrane oxygenator. C: False — VA-ECMO is a short-term device (typically ≤21 days); destination therapy employs implantable long-term LVADs. D: True — Moderate-or-greater AR is a contraindication, as with TandemHeart.

questions.question: Regarding pharmacological management of acute decompensated heart failure (ADHF) according to ESC guidelines:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Inotropic agents are recommended as routine first-line therapy in all patients admitted with acute decompensated heart failure.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Beta-blockers should be initiated within 24 hours of ADHF admission to reduce short-term mortality.

statements.label: C
statements.text: In cardiogenic shock, vasopressors may be required alongside inotropes to maintain adequate organ perfusion pressure.

statements.label: D
statements.text: In 'wet-warm' patients (congestion without haemodynamic compromise), IV loop diuretics are first-line treatment.

questions.topic: ESC guidelines inotropes wet-warm beta-blocker vasopressors cardiogenic shock
questions.explanation: A: False — Inotropes are reserved for the 'cold'/cardiogenic-shock phenotype; they are not routine for all AHF and carry risks including arrhythmia and increased myocardial oxygen demand. B: False — Beta-blockers are withheld or dose-reduced during acute decompensation; they are disease-modifying agents started only after clinical stabilisation. C: True — Vasopressors maintain perfusion pressure in refractory shock alongside inotropes. D: True — Diuretics ± vasodilators are first-line for wet-warm patients.

questions.question: Regarding non-invasive ventilation (NIV) in acute heart failure:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Reduced level of consciousness (low GCS) in the context of acute heart failure is an indication for NIV rather than intubation.

statements.label: B
statements.text: BiPAP has demonstrated superior reduction in hospital mortality over CPAP in randomised trials of acute heart failure.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Initiation of positive pressure ventilation may increase RV afterload through elevated alveolar pressures and increased pulmonary vascular resistance.

statements.label: D
statements.text: CPAP reduces LV afterload by decreasing left ventricular transmural pressure.

questions.topic: CPAP BiPAP NIV afterload RV mortality AHF airway compromise intubation
questions.explanation: A: False — Depressed sensorium and airway compromise are mandatory indications for intubation, not NIV. B: False — BiPAP improves CO2 clearance but has NOT shown mortality superiority over CPAP in AHF (3CPO trial). C: True — Elevated alveolar pressures increase pulmonary vascular resistance and RV afterload — an important haemodynamic consideration. D: True — Positive intrathoracic pressure reduces LV transmural pressure, lowering afterload.

questions.question: Regarding biomarkers in the assessment of acute heart failure:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Markedly elevated troponin in a patient with acute heart failure may indicate an ischaemic precipitant requiring urgent coronary evaluation.

statements.label: B
statements.text: B-type natriuretic peptide (BNP) is released in response to increased ventricular wall stress and volume overload.

statements.label: C
statements.text: A normal BNP reliably excludes the diagnosis of acute heart failure in all clinical contexts.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Obesity is associated with elevated BNP levels, increasing the risk of false-positive heart failure diagnosis in obese patients.

questions.topic: BNP obesity falsely low troponin ischaemic precipitant diagnosis
questions.explanation: A: True — Troponin elevation prompts consideration of ACS as a precipitant. B: True — BNP is released in response to myocardial wall stress. C: False — BNP may be falsely low in obesity, early flash pulmonary oedema, and certain right heart failure states. D: False — Obesity is associated with LOWER BNP levels (increased clearance via adipose natriuretic peptide receptors), risking false-negative rather than false-positive results.

questions.question: Regarding indications and strategies for short-term mechanical circulatory support (MCS) in acute heart failure:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Contraindications to anticoagulation, severe peripheral vascular disease, and the presence of a mechanical aortic valve replacement may preclude the use of certain short-term MCS devices.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Early institution of MCS before requiring multiple vasopressors to prevent multi-organ failure is supported by robust RCT evidence.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Before initiating short-term MCS, an exit strategy (bridge to decision, transplant, bridge, or recovery) should ideally be defined in advance.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Short-term MCS is indicated in cardiogenic shock refractory to medical therapy or in patients in whom medical therapy is expected to be ineffective or harmful.

questions.topic: MCS indications exit strategy RCT evidence age contraindication
questions.explanation: A: True — These are recognised factors that may rule out certain devices, distinct from age, which is only a relative consideration with varying institutional thresholds. B: False — This approach is 'theoretically attractive but requires testing in randomised controlled trials' per the module; it is not yet evidence-based. C: True — A pre-defined exit strategy should be established before implantation. D: True — This is the ESC-aligned indication for short-term MCS.

questions.question: Regarding cardiac transplantation in end-stage heart failure:

statements.label: A
statements.text: In the UK, patients dependent on VA-ECMO or short-term ventricular assist devices are classified as 'super-urgent' priority for organ allocation.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Irreversible fixed pulmonary hypertension is a contraindication due to the risk of acute RV failure in the donor heart.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Age greater than 65 years is an absolute contraindication to cardiac transplantation.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Long-term continuous-flow LVADs provide equivalent 5-year survival to cardiac transplantation in all patient groups.

questions.topic: cardiac transplantation contraindications age relative pulmonary hypertension allocation
questions.explanation: A: True — UK allocation hierarchy: super-urgent (ECMO/VAD) > urgent (inotropes/IABP) > routine. B: True — Fixed pulmonary hypertension risks acute RV failure in the donor heart (right ventricle adapted to normal pulmonary pressures). C: False — Age >65 is a RELATIVE marker of likely comorbidity, not an absolute universal contraindication — an important EDIC trap. D: False — 2–3 year survival can approach transplant in selected patients, but longer-term outcomes with LVADs remain inferior and morbidity is higher.

questions.question: Regarding the left ventricular pressure-volume loop in acute heart failure:

statements.label: A
statements.text: The area enclosed by the pressure-volume loop primarily reflects cardiac afterload rather than myocardial oxygen consumption.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Peripheral VA-ECMO reduces LV stroke work by decreasing the area of the pressure-volume loop.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Load-independent LV contractility (Emax) is reflected by the slope of the end-systolic pressure-volume relationship under varying loading conditions.

statements.label: D
statements.text: The width of the pressure-volume loop (difference between EDV and ESV) represents the stroke volume.

questions.topic: LV pressure-volume loop stroke volume Emax oxygen consumption VA-ECMO
questions.explanation: A: False — The PV loop area (pressure-volume area) is closely correlated with myocardial oxygen consumption (stroke work + potential energy), not afterload directly. B: False — Peripheral VA-ECMO INCREASES LV afterload via retrograde aortic return, potentially increasing the PV loop area (greater stroke work); it is Impella and TandemHeart that reduce LV stroke work by direct LV unloading. C: True — Emax (slope of ESPVR) = load-independent contractility. D: True — Width = stroke volume.

questions.question: The figure compares three left ventricular pressure-volume loops: Panel A (steady state), Panel B (acute myocardial infarction), and Panel C (cardiogenic shock). A patient progresses from an uncomplicated anterior STEMI, as in Panel B, into florid cardiogenic shock six hours later, as in Panel C. Compared to the Panel B loop, which change best characterises the Panel C loop?
options: A: A leftward shift with reduced LVEDV and a narrower loop, reflecting improved diastolic filling
B: A rightward shift with increased LVEDV, further-reduced Emax, and an enlarged pressure-volume area reflecting greater oxygen demand
C: An unchanged loop position with an isolated reduction in stroke volume only
D: Normalisation of Emax toward the steady-state slope as compensatory mechanisms restore contractility
E: A loop essentially indistinguishable from the acute MI state, since the two conditions produce near-identical pressure-volume relationships
questions.explanation: Cardiogenic shock (the Panel C loop) is displaced markedly to the right with significantly increased LVEDV, more severely reduced Emax than the AMI loop, and an enlarged pressure-volume area indicating increased myocardial oxygen demand despite reduced stroke volume. This distinguishes the magnitude of shock from the more modest contractile impairment of uncomplicated AMI (Panel B), where LV pressure and stroke volume may be preserved or only mildly reduced.
questions.topic: PV loop AMI vs cardiogenic shock magnitude Emax LVEDV oxygen demand

questions.question: A patient with cardiogenic shock is established on peripheral VA-ECMO. The figure illustrates the resulting effect on the LV pressure-volume loop in Panel C compared to baseline. Based on the loop change shown, which best describes this haemodynamic effect?
options: A: The loop shifts leftward with reduced end-diastolic volume, consistent with improved ventricular filling
B: The loop area remains similar or increases with a steeper Ea line, consistent with increased arterial elastance and afterload without effective LV unloading
C: The enclosed loop area is reduced and the Ea line is flattened, consistent with effective LV unloading and reduced myocardial oxygen consumption
D: Stroke volume increases substantially while the Ea line becomes shallower, consistent with combined inotropic and vasodilatory support
E: No discernible change from the baseline loop is shown, indicating a haemodynamically neutral effect
questions.explanation: Panel C shows the characteristic VA-ECMO pattern: increased arterial elastance (steeper Ea) reflecting increased afterload, with the loop failing to shrink — stroke work and myocardial oxygen consumption remain unchanged or increase. This contrasts with the pLVAD panel (Impella/TandemHeart), which shows genuine loop-area reduction from direct LV unloading. Peripheral VA-ECMO returns blood retrograde into the aorta and does not vent the LV, which is why adjunctive unloading strategies (IABP, Impella, surgical vent) are often required alongside it.
questions.topic: VA-ECMO PV loop afterload Ea arterial elastance no unloading Panel C

questions.question: A 76-year-old man with a background of COPD is admitted with new-onset acute heart failure. On admission he has a systolic BP of 96 mmHg, community-acquired pneumonia, and an eGFR of 28 mL/min/1.73m². A colleague states his overall risk profile is unremarkable for a first presentation. Which combination of features from this vignette is most strongly associated with increased mortality in acute heart failure registries?
options: A: Male sex is not an independent predictor of mortality in acute heart failure
B: New (de novo) presentation, which registries show carries a uniformly worse prognosis than decompensation of chronic heart failure
C: Advanced age, male sex, low systolic blood pressure, renal dysfunction, and pneumonia
D: An eGFR of 28 mL/min/1.73m² is within the expected range for a 76-year-old and does not represent a risk factor
E: COPD alone, independent of the other clinical features
questions.explanation: Age, male sex, low systolic BP, renal dysfunction, and pneumonia are all established predictors of poor outcome in acute heart failure registries. This patient embodies several of these simultaneously. COPD is a common comorbidity in AHF populations but is not itself among the specific outcome predictors identified in the registries. Male sex — not female — is the predictor identified in registry data, and de novo presentation is not shown to carry a uniformly worse prognosis than decompensated chronic heart failure.
questions.topic: AHF epidemiology predictors poor outcome age renal pneumonia registries

questions.question: A mechanically ventilated patient with severe ARDS (PaO2/FiO2 115, PEEP 14 cmH2O) develops progressive hypotension. Bedside TTE shows a dilated RV with D-shaped interventricular septum and septal dyskinesia. Pulse pressure variation (PPV) is 19%. What does this finding most likely indicate?
options: A: Severe LV systolic dysfunction as the primary haemodynamic problem
B: RV afterload dependency — PPV reflects failing RV output, not volume responsiveness
C: Hypovolaemia requiring a 500 ml fluid bolus to restore cardiac output
D: Cardiac tamponade requiring urgent pericardiocentesis
E: Excessive PEEP compressing the RV and requiring immediate PEEP reduction
questions.explanation: In patients with RV dysfunction on positive pressure ventilation, elevated PPV reflects beat-to-beat variation due to RV afterload dependency, NOT fluid responsiveness. Administering fluids misled by this finding can severely worsen RV dilatation, tricuspid regurgitation and ventricular interdependence — a recognised and dangerous clinical pitfall. The D-shaped septum with septal dyskinesia confirms cor pulmonale. Reducing PEEP in this context may worsen hypoxia and further increase PVR via hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction, rather than address the underlying RV afterload dependency.
questions.section: The RV in Health and Disease
questions.topic: RVF ARDS pulse-pressure-variation heart-lung-interaction ventricular-interdependence

questions.question: A 61-year-old woman is brought to the ED in cardiogenic shock. BP is 72/44 mmHg and has been persistently hypotensive for 25 minutes without another identifiable cause. ECG shows S1Q3T3 pattern with right bundle branch block. Bedside TTE demonstrates a dilated, hypokinetic RV with paradoxical septal motion. CT-PA confirms massive bilateral PE. She has no contraindications to thrombolysis. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: A: Systemic thrombolysis
B: Catheter-directed thrombolysis via interventional radiology
C: Emergency surgical embolectomy
D: Therapeutic anticoagulation with LMWH and close monitoring in the ICU
E: Milrinone infusion to improve RV contractility while awaiting imaging review
questions.explanation: This is a high-risk (massive) PE defined by sustained haemodynamic compromise (BP drop >40 mmHg for >15 min without another cause).
