questions.question: Regarding the management of acute left ventricular dysfunction in the setting of hypertensive emergency:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Labetalol is particularly effective in hypertensive emergency with diastolic dysfunction as the reduction in heart rate facilitates LV filling

statements.label: B
statements.text: Hypertensive pulmonary oedema is more commonly due to diastolic dysfunction than systolic dysfunction, with most patients having preserved LV ejection fraction

statements.label: C
statements.text: Nitroprusside is effective only in systolic LV dysfunction and should be avoided in diastolic dysfunction due to excessive preload reduction

statements.label: D
statements.text: Diuretics are safe and should be administered aggressively early in all patients with hypertensive emergency and pulmonary oedema to reduce filling pressures

questions.topic: LV-dysfunction diastolic systolic pulmonary-oedema labetalol diuretics nitroprusside preserved-EF
questions.explanation: A: True — By reducing HR, labetalol increases diastolic filling time, directly addressing the mechanism of diastolic LV dysfunction. B: True — The majority of hypertensive pulmonary oedema episodes are due to diastolic dysfunction — the hypertrophied, non-compliant LV fails to fill adequately at the acutely elevated pressure; LVEF is often normal or preserved. C: False — Nitroprusside (arterial and venous dilator) reduces both preload and afterload and IS effective regardless of systolic or diastolic aetiology — it should not be avoided in diastolic dysfunction. D: False — Diuretics should be used CAUTIOUSLY in diastolic dysfunction to avoid excessive reduction of the filling pressures needed to fill the non-compliant ventricle; aggressive diuresis risks swinging from pulmonary oedema to renal failure.

questions.question: Regarding blood pressure measurement in clinical practice:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Blood pressure cuff width should be approximately 80% of the arm circumference, with the cuff length encircling 40% of the arm

statements.label: B
statements.text: Automated oscillometric blood pressure devices are equally reliable in haemodynamically unstable patients and in patients with arrhythmias as in normotensive patients in sinus rhythm

statements.label: C
statements.text: Systolic pressure corresponds to phase 5 Korotkoff sounds (disappearance of sounds) and diastolic to phase 1 (first appearance)

statements.label: D
statements.text: A cuff that is too small for the patient's arm circumference produces a falsely elevated blood pressure reading

questions.topic: BP-measurement Korotkoff cuff-size oscillometric intra-arterial-monitoring arrhythmia
questions.explanation: A: False — The correct dimensions are cuff width 40% and length 80% of arm circumference, not the reverse — an incorrectly proportioned cuff produces inaccurate readings. B: False — Automated oscillometric devices are specifically LESS accurate in common ICU situations: shock, arrhythmias, and mechanical ventilation. Intra-arterial monitoring should be considered for patients requiring potent antihypertensive agents. C: False — Phase 1 (first appearance of sounds) corresponds to systolic pressure; phase 5 (disappearance) corresponds to diastolic — not the reverse. D: True — An undersized cuff (or one applied too loosely) overestimates blood pressure — the most common error in manual BP measurement.

questions.question: Regarding the pathophysiology and cerebral autoregulation in hypertensive emergencies:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Acute increase in systemic vascular resistance — mediated by catecholamines, α-adrenergic activity and RAAS activation — is the most common pathophysiological mechanism in hypertensive crises

statements.label: B
statements.text: The cerebral autoregulatory curve is shifted to higher MAP values in patients with chronic hypertension, meaning a 'normal' MAP may be below their threshold for adequate cerebral perfusion

statements.label: C
statements.text: Once elevated, blood pressure should always be treated aggressively regardless of the clinical context, as persistence of hypertension invariably worsens neurological outcomes in stroke

statements.label: D
statements.text: Previously normotensive patients (e.g. acute glomerulonephritis, cocaine-induced, preeclampsia) only develop end-organ injury once diastolic blood pressure exceeds 150 mmHg, similar to chronically hypertensive patients

questions.topic: cerebral-autoregulation SVR RAAS normotensive preeclampsia stroke penumbra autoregulatory-shift
questions.explanation: A: True — Acute SVR increase is the primary mechanism in most hypertensive crises; RAAS and adrenergic activation are the key mediators, with fluid overload a less common contributor. B: True — This rightward shift of the autoregulatory curve is the physiological reason blood pressure should NOT be normalised acutely in hypertensive emergencies. C: False — This is a dangerous overstatement: in ischaemic stroke, elevated BP is partly compensatory to maintain cerebral perfusion pressure against raised ICP; aggressive universal lowering can worsen the penumbra, and specific (not universal) BP targets apply. D: False — Previously normotensive individuals have a normal (non-shifted) autoregulatory curve and develop end-organ injury at the much lower threshold of DBP 100–110 mmHg, not only above 150 mmHg — they do NOT require the same high threshold as chronically hypertensive patients.

questions.question: A 68-year-old man with poorly controlled hypertension presents with sudden collapse and reduced consciousness. BP is 210/126 mmHg. A non-contrast CT head is obtained (see figure). Based on the typical distribution of hypertensive intracerebral haemorrhage, which anatomical region is most characteristically affected, as illustrated here?
options: A: Lobar (cortical-subcortical) haemorrhage, the pattern most typical of cerebral amyloid angiopathy rather than hypertension
B: Basal ganglia/thalamic region — the classic site for hypertensive intracerebral haemorrhage
C: Isolated cerebellar haemorrhage without supratentorial extension
D: Extradural haematoma from head trauma
E: Isolated subarachnoid haemorrhage from aneurysmal rupture
questions.explanation: Hypertensive intracerebral haemorrhage classically occurs in the basal ganglia/thalamic region (as shown here) and the cerebellum, reflecting rupture of small perforating arteries (e.g. lenticulostriate vessels) chronically weakened by hypertensive vasculopathy (Charcot-Bouchard aneurysms/lipohyalinosis). This contrasts with lobar haemorrhage, which is more typically associated with cerebral amyloid angiopathy, particularly in older normotensive or mildly hypertensive patients.
questions.topic: intracerebral haemorrhage basal ganglia hypertensive bleed CT imaging location

questions.question: The figure shows cerebral blood flow autoregulation curves for normotensive individuals and patients with chronic hypertension. A chronically hypertensive patient in a hypertensive emergency has their MAP acutely reduced from 180 mmHg to 90 mmHg — a level that would be entirely well-tolerated in a normotensive individual. Based on the curves shown, what is the most likely physiological consequence?
options: A: Cerebral hyperperfusion and rising intracranial pressure, since 90 mmHg still exceeds the lower autoregulatory threshold in this patient
B: The autoregulatory curve for chronic hypertension is identical to the normotensive curve once blood pressure has been elevated for more than a few weeks
C: No change in cerebral blood flow is possible outside the initial measured pressure range shown on the curve

D: Cerebral ischaemia, because 90 mmHg falls below the rightward-shifted lower limit of autoregulation in chronic hypertension, even though it would be within the plateau for a normotensive individual
E: No adverse effect, since 90 mmHg lies within the autoregulatory plateau for all individuals regardless of chronic BP history
questions.explanation: The autoregulatory curve is shifted rightward (to higher pressures) in chronic hypertension. A MAP of 90 mmHg falls within the flat autoregulatory plateau for a normotensive individual, maintaining stable cerebral blood flow — but the same 90 mmHg lies below the rightward-shifted lower limit of autoregulation in the chronically hypertensive patient, where cerebral blood flow becomes pressure-dependent and falls, risking cerebral ischaemia. This is precisely why hypertensive emergencies are managed by reducing MAP by only 20-25% rather than normalising blood pressure.
questions.topic: cerebral autoregulation curve shift chronic hypertension MAP reduction ischaemia

questions.question: A 58-year-old man presents with acute severe tearing chest pain radiating to the back, BP 195/115 mmHg, and a pulse deficit between the two arms. The figure shows a cardiac MRI obtained to confirm the diagnosis. Based on the segment of the aorta involved in the dissection shown, which management pathway is indicated?
options: A: Type A dissection — since it involves the ascending aorta, medical management alone is preferred to avoid surgical risk
B: The imaging finding is non-specific for dissection type and further invasive angiography is mandatory before any treatment decision can be made
C: Type A dissection — urgent surgical repair is required, with medical BP/HR control as a bridge to theatre
D: Type B dissection — medical management with a target SBP 100-110 mmHg and HR 60 bpm is definitive therapy
E: The ascending aorta involvement makes this a Type B dissection requiring endovascular stenting
questions.explanation: The MRI demonstrates an intimal flap involving the ascending aorta — a Type A dissection by the Stanford classification. Type A (proximal) dissections require urgent surgical repair because of the high risk of complications including aortic rupture, cardiac tamponade, and acute aortic regurgitation; medical therapy (β-blockade ± vasodilator, targeting SBP 100-110 mmHg and HR 60 bpm) is instituted immediately as a bridge to theatre, not as definitive treatment. Type B (distal, descending aorta only) dissections are usually managed medically unless complicated.
questions.topic: aortic dissection type A ascending aorta MRI imaging surgical management Stanford classification

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT among the recognised precipitating factors for perioperative hypertension that should be assessed and corrected before instituting antihypertensive drug therapy?
options: A: Postoperative pain
B: Bladder distension
C: Elevated haemoglobin
D: Hypercapnia
E: Hypothermia
questions.explanation: Recognised precipitating factors for perioperative hypertension include pain, anxiety, altered blood gases (hypoxaemia, hypercapnia, acidosis), hypervolaemia, bladder distension, and hypothermia — all of which should be assessed and corrected before considering antihypertensive drug therapy. Preoperative haemoglobin concentration is not among the specifically recognised precipitants in this context. Perioperative hypertension is most commonly associated with neurosurgical and vascular procedures (30-80% incidence) and can occur in up to 25% of noncardiac surgical patients; postoperative hypertension is usually self-limited (2-6 hours), favouring short-acting titratable agents.
questions.section: Management of Specific Conditions: Catecholamine Excess and Perioperative
questions.topic: perioperative hypertension precipitating factors pain bladder distension hypothermia incidence

questions.question: A 74-year-old woman with longstanding hypertension presents with acute pulmonary oedema and BP 210/122 mmHg. Chest radiograph shows pulmonary oedema without significant cardiomegaly. ECG shows voltage criteria for left ventricular hypertrophy. Fundoscopy reveals flame haemorrhages and cotton wool spots. Which underlying mechanism of acute left ventricular dysfunction is most consistent with this presentation?
options: A: Diastolic dysfunction, from a non-compliant hypertrophied ventricle with impaired filling
B: Right ventricular failure secondary to acute pulmonary embolism
C: Systolic dysfunction with reduced ejection fraction and ventricular dilatation
D: Acute papillary muscle rupture causing severe mitral regurgitation
E: High-output cardiac failure from a hyperdynamic circulation
questions.explanation: This presentation matches the recognised clinical pattern of diastolic (not systolic) LV dysfunction in hypertensive emergency: higher BP elevation, significant hypertensive retinopathy (flame haemorrhages, cotton wool spots), and LVH on ECG, WITHOUT cardiomegaly on CXR. The hypertrophied, non-compliant ventricle fails to fill adequately at the acutely elevated afterload, precipitating pulmonary oedema despite preserved (often normal) ejection fraction — the majority of hypertensive pulmonary oedema episodes are of this type. Systolic dysfunction, by contrast, typically presents with a lower BP elevation, absent retinopathy, and four-chamber cardiomegaly on CXR.
questions.topic: diastolic dysfunction systolic dysfunction hypertensive pulmonary oedema clinical clues LVH retinopathy
